name: V8 Maglev
summary: V8's mid-tier JIT IR: single-level SSA with dense indexed operands, fewer node kinds than TurboFan's sea-of-nodes, explicit deopt info inline at each speculative op. Trades top-tier expressiveness for compile speed; bails to Sparkplug rather than to the interpreter.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Maglev's IR is single-level SSA with dense indexed operands, fewer node kinds than TurboFan's sea-of-nodes, and explicit deopt info inline at each speculative op. The data model is intentionally less expressive than TurboFan; the trade-off favours compile speed over peak quality.

sections.title: Mid-tier between Sparkplug and TurboFan
sections.content:
Failed speculations in Maglev bail to Sparkplug (V8's baseline JIT) rather than to the interpreter, which is why Maglev's deopt metadata records *baseline-frame* state rather than full bytecode-pointer state — the deopt layout is co-designed with the tier directly below it.

The single-level shape (no separate LIR) and the linear node table both reflect the same priority: keep the IR small enough that compile time fits between a function getting hot and the user noticing latency.

sections.title: Design position in V8's tier ladder
sections.content:
Maglev's place in V8's tier pipeline (between Sparkplug and TurboFan/Turboshaft) is a deliberate response to two forces: TurboFan's compile time is too high for transiently-hot code, while Sparkplug's template-JIT output is too slow for sustained throughput. Maglev sits in the middle — fast enough to compile aggressively, good enough to run reasonably warm code without invoking the top tier.

This tier-stacking pattern (interpreter → baseline → mid-tier → top-tier) has converged across modern JIT engines (V8 Maglev/TurboFan/Turboshaft, JavaScriptCore Baseline/DFG/FTL, HotSpot C1/C2). Maglev is V8's specific answer to the mid-tier slot.
